title: Information transfer along the causal lightcone of a brickwork quantum circuit
abstract: Understanding how local information propagates through many-body quantum systems is a central problem in nonequilibrium dynamics, with important implications for quantum communication, state transfer, and remote sensing. In this work, we investigate information transfer along a one-dimensional open chain of qudits, focusing on the task of recovering information initially encoded at one end via measurements performed at the opposite end. By restricting the dynamics to brickwork quantum circuits, and considering small $M$-qudit subsystems on the causal ``lightcone'' of the circuit, we obtain several results valid even for large system sizes $N$ or for nonintegrable global dynamics. Within this framework, lossless information transfer is linked to the existence of peripheral eigenvalues of a quantum channel $Φ_M$, which governs the evolution of the $M$-qudit local subsystem along the lightcone. We investigate conditions under which brickwork circuits admit such peripheral eigenvalues. For qubit chains and $M=1$, we show that the dual-unitary property is necessary, whereas for larger local subsystems ($M \geq 2$) or higher-dimensional qudits, this requirement can be relaxed. Perhaps surprisingly, we can use the peripheral eigenvalue condition to construct examples exhibiting lossless information transfer through chains of arbitrary system size $N$, even when the underlying circuit dynamics is nonintegrable and thermalising at long times.
summary: This paper identifies that the ability to transfer information without loss through a quantum circuit depends on the presence of peripheral eigenvalues in the evolution channel. While dual-unitarity is required for simple qubit chains, larger subsystems or higher-dimensional qudits can achieve lossless transfer even in chaotic, thermalizing regimes.
main_problem: Investigating the conditions for lossless versus lossy information transfer and remote sensing of parameters along the causal lightcone of a brickwork quantum circuit.
main_result: Lossless information transfer is linked to the existence of peripheral eigenvalues (unit modulus) in the quantum channel, a requirement that is necessary for qubits (M=1) but can be relaxed for larger subsystems or higher-dimensional qudits, even in chaotic, thermalizing systems.
method: The authors use a quantum channel framework ($\Phi_M$) to map many-body dynamics to the lightcone, employing spectral analysis of the channel, numerical optimization, and analytical constructions using the Poincaré recurrence theorem and Weyl's inequality.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional open chain of $N$ qudits (qubits or qutrits) undergoing Floquet dynamics driven by a brickwork quantum circuit of alternating unitary layers.
key_observables: Trace distance (distinguishability), Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), information transfer ratios ($\eta_D, \eta_F$), and operator linear entropy.
important_parameters: Subsystem size $M$, local dimension $d$, non-local gate parameters ($J_x, J_y, J_z$), and the presence of peripheral eigenvalues ($|z|=1$).
main_assumptions: The system is assumed to be in a high-temperature/maximally mixed state outside the encoded subsystem, and the dynamics are restricted to the brickwork circuit class.
figures_summary: Schematics of information encoding/recovery; plots of eigenvalue spectra and information decay rates; numerical optimization of gate parameters; and entanglement entropy analysis showing an absence of many-body scars.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of information propagation, defines the brickwork circuit and quantum channel framework, analyzes the spectral conditions for information loss/preservation, extends the findings to larger subsystems and higher dimensions, and concludes by demonstrating that these lossless properties can coexist with quantum chaos and ETH.
why_interesting: It provides a surprising link between the existence of non-dissipative information channels and quantum chaos, showing that thermalizing, non-integrable systems can still support perfect information transfer, which is highly relevant for quantum communication and remote sensing in many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Some information λ is initially encoded in the state of the first M qudits (the red, blue, green, purple boxes, for M = 1, 2, 3, 4, respectively), and estimated through measurement of the last M qudits at a later time. FIG. 4. Level spacing statistics of the Floquet unitary ˆU, built with a Haar-random local unitary gate ˆU. The distribution of level spacings is consistent with that of the circular unitary ensemble (CUE).

FIG. 3. Distribution of |zmax| (the non-trivial eigenvalue of ΦM( ˆU) with largest magnitude) for an ensemble of 10000 Haar-random unitaries ˆU. Peripheral eigenvalues (|zmax| = 1) are extremely rare. The inset shows the mean of each distri- bution as a function of M, which saturates at a value smaller than unity as M increases.
